home.title: Choose a Ceremony
home.subtitle: Select the ceremony you want to facilitate

ceremony.source: Based on: Scrum Guide · ICAgile Workbook · Scrum & Kanban

planning.name: Sprint Planning
planning.desc: Align the team on the Sprint Goal and plan the work

steps.title: Define Sprint Goal
steps.why: The Sprint Goal gives the team a shared purpose and a north star for the Sprint. Without a clear goal, teams tend to work in silos. The Product Owner must commit to this goal before planning continues.

steps.title: Backlog Review
steps.why: The team reviews the top backlog items with the Product Owner to clarify acceptance criteria and dependencies. Items must be understood well enough to estimate before they can be selected.

steps.title: Capacity Check
steps.why: The team calculates available capacity in hours or story points for this Sprint, accounting for holidays, support duties, and known absences. Overcommitting leads to missed Sprint Goals.

steps.title: Story Breakdown & Estimation
steps.why: Breaking stories into tasks reveals hidden complexity and enables realistic commitment. Estimation as a team surfaces differing understandings and drives alignment.

steps.title: Team Commitment
steps.why: The team explicitly commits to the Sprint Goal — not to the task list. This is a moment of shared accountability, not a contract. Ask: 'Is there anything that would prevent us from achieving this goal?'

tips: Start with the Sprint Goal, not the backlog. Goal first, tasks second.
Timebox backlog refinement ruthlessly — rabbit holes kill planning sessions.
If estimation takes more than 10 minutes on one story, it's a signal to split it.

daily.name: Daily Stand-up
daily.desc: Synchronise the team and surface blockers

steps.title: What did I do yesterday?
steps.why: This question surfaces progress toward the Sprint Goal, not just task completion. Encourage participants to connect their work to the goal, not just list activities.

steps.title: What will I do today?
steps.why: Today's plan creates micro-commitments that the team can hold each other to. It also surfaces dependencies — if A needs B's output today, both need to know.

steps.title: Any blockers?
steps.why: Blockers identified here get resolved outside the stand-up, not during it. The Scrum Master's job is to capture and action these immediately after the meeting ends.

tips: Stand up. Literally. It keeps the meeting short.
Talk to the board, not to the Scrum Master. This is a team sync, not a status report.
If a conversation starts, park it. 'Let's take that offline' — then do it.

review.name: Sprint Review
review.desc: Inspect the increment and adapt the backlog with stakeholders

steps.title: Sprint Goal Recap
steps.why: Remind everyone what the team committed to. The demo should be evaluated against the Sprint Goal, not against individual stories. Did the team achieve what they set out to do?

steps.title: Demo Completed Work
steps.why: Only demo working software — no slides, no mockups. The demo is the proof of Done. Stakeholders should interact with the product where possible.

steps.title: Stakeholder Feedback
steps.why: This is the primary purpose of the Review: inspecting the increment with people outside the team. Feedback here directly shapes the next Sprint. Capture it on the backlog immediately.

steps.title: Backlog Update
steps.why: The Product Owner updates the backlog in real time based on feedback. New items, changed priorities, and dropped items should all be reflected before the session ends.

steps.title: Next Sprint Preview
steps.why: A brief look ahead — not planning — gives stakeholders context for what's coming. It also creates continuity between Reviews and avoids 'what are you working on?' conversations.

tips: Demo to the Sprint Goal, not to the story list. Start with: 'Our goal was X. Here's what we built.'
Invite real stakeholders, not just the Product Owner. External perspective is the point.
Capture feedback as backlog items immediately — don't rely on memory.

retro.name: Retrospective
retro.desc: Inspect and adapt the team's way of working

steps.title: Set the Stage
steps.why: Psychological safety is the prerequisite for a useful retrospective. Prime the team with a check-in (one-word how-are-you, or a quick energiser) to shift out of delivery mode and into reflection mode.

steps.title: Gather Data — What went well & To improve
steps.why: Surface facts and feelings from the Sprint before interpreting them. The board captures the raw data. Avoid jumping to solutions — gather first, discuss second.

steps.title: Generate Insights
steps.why: Look for patterns in the data: which issues are recurring? Which successes can be reinforced? Dot-vote or group notes to find the highest-signal items for discussion.

steps.title: Decide What to Do — Actions
steps.why: Actions must be specific, owned, and achievable within the next Sprint. 'We should communicate better' is not an action. 'Alice will share the deployment checklist by Wednesday' is.

steps.title: Close the Retrospective
steps.why: A brief close — one word to describe the session, appreciation round, or just a thank-you — signals that the team's time was valued. It also anchors the action items.

tips: No blame, no names. Talk about systems and situations, not people.
End with no more than 2–3 actions. A long list means nothing gets done.
Review last Sprint's actions first — did they help? Accountability drives change.
